description: Boilermaker specialty knowledge — boiler tube replacement, pressure vessel work, heat exchanger bundle pulling, refractory installation, ASME Section I and VIII code requirements, and NBIC repairs. For boilermakers working under the Pipe Fitting trade.

waterwall_tubes.description: Tubes forming the furnace walls of a water-tube boiler. Carry water/steam mixture. Exposed to direct flame radiation.
waterwall_tubes.materials: SA-210 Grade A1 (carbon steel) for most waterwalls. SA-213 T11 or T22 for higher temperature zones.
waterwall_tubes.sizes: Typically 2 inch to 3 inch OD, various wall thicknesses.
replacement_procedure: Identify damaged tube(s) — UT thickness survey, visual inspection for bulges/cracks
Cut out damaged section — leave straight, clean ends for fit-up
Prepare replacement tube — match material, OD, wall thickness exactly
Bevel tube ends per WPS (typically 37.5 degree bevel, 1/16 land, 1/16 gap)
Fit-up with internal alignment clamp or external jig
Purge with argon for alloy tubes (T11, T22)
Weld root pass GTAW, fill and cap SMAW or GTAW
NDE per code — typically RT or UT for waterwall tube butt welds
waterwall_tubes.window_cuts: When access is limited, cut a window (rectangular opening) in adjacent tubes to reach the damaged tube. After repair, weld window tubes back with full penetration welds. Very labor-intensive.
waterwall_tubes.common_mistake: Not matching tube material exactly. Putting carbon steel tube in an alloy zone or vice versa. Always verify material with PMI before welding.

economizer_tubes.description: Finned tubes that preheat feedwater using flue gas. Located in the cooler gas path.
economizer_tubes.material: SA-210 A1 (carbon steel). Fins are carbon steel welded or extruded.
economizer_tubes.failure_modes: Erosion from fly ash (especially at tube bends), cold-end corrosion (sulfuric acid condensation below dewpoint), FAC (flow accelerated corrosion) on inside.
economizer_tubes.replacement: Often replaced in banks (multiple tubes at once). Tubes are pre-fabricated in shop with fins, shipped to field, and welded in place.

superheater_reheater_tubes.description: Tubes carrying steam at highest temperature in the boiler. Subject to creep, oxidation, and fireside corrosion.
superheater_reheater_tubes.materials: SA-213 T11, T22, T91 (for highest temperature sections). TP304H, TP321H (austenitic stainless for extreme conditions).
superheater_reheater_tubes.failure_modes: Creep rupture (long-term high-temperature exposure), fireside corrosion (alkali sulfates, vanadium attack), steam-side oxidation (exfoliation scale).
superheater_reheater_tubes.tube_sampling: Periodically remove tube samples for metallurgical analysis — measure remaining creep life, wall thickness, internal scale thickness.
superheater_reheater_tubes.CRITICAL: Dissimilar metal welds (DMW) between ferritic and austenitic tubes require special procedures — Inconel 82/182 filler, or direct P91-to-TP347H with proper procedure. DMW failures are common and costly.

tube_rolling.description: Expanding tube into tube sheet hole using a roller expander tool. Creates a mechanical seal between tube and tube sheet.
procedure: Clean tube sheet holes — no corrosion, scale, or debris
Insert tube through tube sheet — verify projection per spec (typically 1/8 inch beyond tube sheet face)
Insert roller expander tool into tube end
Expand tube using torque-controlled roller — expand to specified wall reduction (typically 5-8% wall thinning)
Measure tube ID after rolling to verify expansion amount
Roll from both ends if tube passes through two tube sheets
tube_rolling.torque_control: Modern tube rollers use torque-controlled drives. Set torque to achieve target wall reduction. Over-rolling thins tube wall excessively and weakens it. Under-rolling allows leakage.
tube_rolling.tube_projection: Tube must project beyond tube sheet face per engineering spec. Too much projection interferes with channel cover. Too little does not allow proper seal weld (if strength-welded).

strength_weld_vs_seal_weld.seal_weld: Light fillet weld around tube-to-tube sheet joint. Prevents leakage but tube is held by rolling. Most common.
strength_weld_vs_seal_weld.strength_weld: Full penetration or high-strength weld. Tube is held by weld, not rolling. Used in high-pressure or high-temperature service.
strength_weld_vs_seal_weld.qualification: Tube-to-tube sheet welding requires separate WPS qualification per ASME IX. Standard pipe WPS does not apply.

tube_plugging.when: Leaking tube that cannot be repaired in place. Plug the tube to isolate it from service. Reduces heat transfer area but allows continued operation.

types.tapered_plug: Solid metal plug driven into tube end with hammer. Simple, fast, temporary. Not code-approved for permanent repair in many jurisdictions.
types.welded_plug: Plug welded into tube sheet. Permanent. Code-approved per NBIC.
types.explosive_plug: Plug expanded by small explosive charge. Fast, no welding. Used where many plugs needed quickly (turnaround work).
types.mechanical_plug: Expanding plug tightened with bolt. Removable. Good for temporary isolation during inspection.

tube_plugging.limit: Most boilers can tolerate 5-10% plugged tubes before derating is required. Engineering must approve plug count and location — too many plugs in one area causes flow maldistribution.

ASME_Section_VIII.Div_1: Design by rules. Most common for process vessels. Covers shells, heads, nozzles, supports, openings.
ASME_Section_VIII.Div_2: Design by analysis. Allows thinner walls through detailed stress analysis. Used for high-pressure or unusual configurations.
ASME_Section_VIII.U_stamp: Manufacturer authorization to build new vessels. Vessel gets U stamp on nameplate.
ASME_Section_VIII.R_stamp: Holder authorization to repair or alter existing vessels per NBIC.

alterations_vs_repairs.repair: Restoring vessel to original design condition. Same material, same thickness, same configuration.
alterations_vs_repairs.alteration: Changing vessel from original design — adding nozzles, re-rating pressure, changing material. Requires engineering review and may require re-stamping.

procedure: Verify new nozzle matches engineering design — size, schedule, flange rating, material
Cut out old nozzle — preserve shell material for examination
Prep shell opening — grind smooth, verify dimensions
Check reinforcement requirements — reinforcing pad or integral reinforcement
Fit new nozzle — align with centerline and projection per drawing
Weld per qualified WPS — typically full penetration weld through shell
If reinforcing pad: install pad, weld to shell and nozzle neck, install telltale hole (weep hole) in pad
NDE per code — RT or UT on shell-to-nozzle weld, PT on pad fillet welds
Hydrotest after all welding complete
nozzle_replacement.telltale_hole: 1/4 inch hole drilled and tapped in reinforcing pad. Allows pressure testing of pad welds and detection of through-wall cracks during service. DO NOT plug this hole — it is a safety device.

vessel_internals.trays: Distillation column trays — sieve, valve, bubble cap. Remove from top through manway. Inspect for corrosion, plugging, mechanical damage.
vessel_internals.baffles: Heat exchanger baffles — guide tube bundle flow. Check for erosion, vibration damage.
vessel_internals.demisters: Wire mesh or vane-type. Remove through manway. Clean or replace if plugged.
vessel_internals.distributors: Liquid distribution devices in columns. Check spray nozzles, weir levels, trough levelness.

procedure: Drain and decontaminate exchanger
Remove channel head cover bolts (or floating head cover)
Remove channel head (for fixed tube sheet: not applicable — bundle does not pull)
Break bundle free — may be stuck from corrosion or scale. Use hydraulic bundle extractor or mechanical puller.
Pull bundle out on rails or rollers. Bundle can weigh tons — proper support essential.
Inspect tube sheet, tubes (OD), baffles, tie rods, spacers
Measure tube wall thickness (UT from OD) on sample tubes
Clean tubes if reusing (hydroblasting, mechanical cleaning)
bundle_pulling.clearance: Need clear space equal to tube length on the pull end. Verify before starting — nothing worse than pulling a bundle halfway out and hitting a pipe rack column.
bundle_pulling.weight: Large bundles can weigh 5-20 tons. Crane or forklift for handling. Support bundle full length — do not let it sag.
bundle_pulling.bundle_extractor: Hydraulic ram that pushes or pulls bundle through shell. Rated capacity must exceed bundle weight plus friction.

tube_sheet.inspection: Check for corrosion, erosion, cracking around tube holes. Measure ligament (web between holes). Measure tube hole diameter — enlarged holes cannot grip tube properly during rolling.
tube_sheet.resurfacing: If tube sheet face is damaged, it can be machined in place (portable lathe) to restore gasket seating surface. Remove minimum material — tube sheet thickness is code-required.

castable.description: Hydraulic-setting refractory concrete. Mixed with water, poured or pumped into forms.
installation: Install anchor system (Y-anchors, V-anchors, hex mesh — welded to shell)
Build forms — steel or wood, coated with release agent
Mix castable per manufacturer — controlled water ratio is critical (too much water = weak refractory)
Pour and vibrate to remove air voids. Do not over-vibrate (causes segregation).
Cure for minimum 24 hours. Keep moist (cover with plastic or wet burlap).
Dry out per controlled schedule — typically: ambient to 250F at 25F/hour, hold 24 hours at 250F, then 25F/hour to 600F, hold 24 hours, then 50F/hour to operating temperature.
CRITICAL: Do not rush dry-out. Trapped moisture flashes to steam and can explosively spall the refractory.
castable.types: Insulating castable (low density, low thermal conductivity), dense castable (high density, abrasion resistant), low-cement castable (LCC — better hot strength)

brick.firebrick: Dense, hard, direct flame exposure. ASTM C155. Mortared joints (air-setting or heat-setting mortar).
brick.insulating_brick: Lightweight, high insulation value. Backs up firebrick. ASTM C155 Group 23-26.
brick.laying_pattern: Alternate header and stretcher courses. Stagger joints. Tight mortar joints — no voids.

refractory.anchor_failure: Most common refractory failure. Anchors corrode (austenitic SS anchors in reducing atmosphere), overheat, or fatigue from thermal cycling. When anchors fail, refractory falls. Inspect anchor tips at every turnaround.

ceramic_fiber.description: Lightweight blanket or module insulation. Used in furnaces, heaters, ducts.
ceramic_fiber.installation: Modules impaled on studs welded to shell. Or blanket layered and pinned with stainless steel pins.
ceramic_fiber.health_hazard: Ceramic fiber is a suspected carcinogen (IARC Group 2B). Respirator required during installation and removal. Minimize airborne fiber.

NBIC_repairs.description: National Board Inspection Code governs repairs and alterations to boilers and pressure vessels.
NBIC_repairs.R_stamp_holder: Only organizations with NBIC R-stamp authorization can perform code repairs. R-stamp requires quality system, authorized inspector access, and National Board registration.
NBIC_repairs.R_1_form: Report of repair — documents what was repaired, materials used, welding procedures, NDE, and pressure test. Signed by repair organization and Authorized Inspector.
NBIC_repairs.R_2_form: Report of alteration — for changes to original design. Requires engineering review.
NBIC_repairs.authorized_inspector: All boiler and pressure vessel repairs must be inspected by an Authorized Inspector (AI) commissioned by the National Board. The AI verifies code compliance and signs the R-form.
NBIC_repairs.jurisdictional_requirements: Each state/province has its own boiler and pressure vessel laws. Some require notification before repair. Some require permits. Know your jurisdiction's requirements before starting work.
